reasoning: The question asks whether the fatalities in a 30-day window will strictly exceed the 30-day average over the preceding 360 days. In conflict event databases like ACLED, fatality counts are typically right-skewed: most months see few fatalities, while a few outlier months (e.g. major clashes in Papua or occasional mass protests/riots) see many. Because the mean is inflated by these outliers, the median is almost always lower than the mean. Consequently, the base rate for a randomly selected future 30-day period strictly exceeding the historical 12-month average is below 50%, typically around 25-35%. The prompt also mentions a recent known average of 0.000—if this reflects a true absence of recent violence or a database pause, the chance of a sudden spike > 0 remains low. In line with forecasting rules, the probabilities are set below 0.5, with uncertainty gradually increasing toward 0.5 for the more distant resolution dates.
